evidence: Adapter tool contracts are inconsistently scoped: `AgentToolsProtocol` aggregates messaging/participants/contacts/memory/schema/dispatch in `src/thenvoi/core/protocols.py`, while multiple adapters still type `tools` as the full protocol (`src/thenvoi/adapters/anthropic.py`, `src/thenvoi/adapters/langgraph.py`, `src/thenvoi/adapters/parlant.py`) even when they only exercise smaller subsets.

Adapter initialization surfaces drift by framework: `CodexAdapter` centralizes options in `CodexAdapterConfig` (`src/thenvoi/adapters/codex.py`) while other adapters expose long optional-argument constructors (`src/thenvoi/adapters/anthropic.py`, `src/thenvoi/adapters/claude_sdk.py`, `src/thenvoi/adapters/crewai.py`, `src/thenvoi/adapters/pydantic_ai.py`).

A concrete contract break exists between helper abstractions and runtime adapters: `run_parlant_agent` passes `model/guidelines/enable_execution_reporting` to `ParlantAdapter` (`src/thenvoi/testing/example_runners.py`), but `ParlantAdapter.__init__` requires `server` and `parlant_agent` and does not accept those kwargs (`src/thenvoi/adapters/parlant.py`).

Test doubles drift from the declared protocol: `ToolSchemaProviderProtocol.get_openai_tool_schemas(..., include_memory=False)` is keyword-only in `src/thenvoi/core/protocols.py`, but codex test fakes implement no `include_memory` parameter (`tests/adapters/test_codex_adapter.py`, `tests/integrations/codex/test_adapter_e2e.py`).

README.md declares multiple integrations as "Production ready" (LangGraph, Pydantic AI, Anthropic SDK, Claude Agent SDK, Codex, CrewAI, Parlant, A2A variants), but pyproject.toml test defaults run `-m 'not integration'`, so the highest-risk cross-system paths are excluded by default.

tests/support/integration/config.py implements strict live-fixture policy enforcement and real API fixtures, but that policy only executes when integration tests are explicitly selected; default test posture does not enforce these runtime contracts.

tests/support/integration/config.py fixture logic depends on ambient external state (e.g., selecting `peers_response.data[0]` and environment-driven cleanup modes), which increases fragility and reduces deterministic repeatability of governance-level contract checks.

Blind packet architecture context marks examples/common/bootstrap.py and examples/prompts/characters.py as high-importer surfaces (16 and 13 importers), but this batch shows no explicit test-contract harness tied to those docs-facing behavior contracts.

`src/thenvoi/adapters/codex.py` concentrates transport lifecycle, thread/session state, local command parsing, approval UX workflow, task-event translation, model fallback, and sandbox policy normalization in one adapter class (~1900 lines with many private control-flow branches).

Multiple adapters repeat the same session-bootstrap/system-update/history-staging shape (`src/thenvoi/adapters/anthropic.py`, `src/thenvoi/adapters/pydantic_ai.py`, `src/thenvoi/adapters/crewai.py`, `src/thenvoi/adapters/langgraph.py`) rather than sharing a single orchestration policy surface.

`src/thenvoi/client/streaming/client.py` and `src/thenvoi/platform/link.py` both implement repetitive per-topic join/leave and event-wrapping methods, creating two layers of near-mechanical channel choreography.

Runner entrypoints duplicate similar environment parsing, prompt composition, mount validation, and startup wiring across `docker/codex/runner.py`, `docker/claude_sdk/runner.py`, and `examples/claude_sdk_docker/runner.py`, with divergent defaults and behavior.

findings.summary: Example runner abstraction is incompatible with ParlantAdapter’s actual constructor contract.
evidence: `run_parlant_agent` constructs `ParlantAdapter(model=..., guidelines=..., enable_execution_reporting=...)` in `src/thenvoi/testing/example_runners.py`.
`ParlantAdapter.__init__` in `src/thenvoi/adapters/parlant.py` requires `server` and `parlant_agent` and has no `model`, `guidelines`, or `enable_execution_reporting` parameters.
findings.suggestion: Unify the public adapter contract: either (a) update `run_parlant_agent` to instantiate a real Parlant server/agent and pass `server`/`parlant_agent`, or (b) introduce a supported factory/wrapper in the adapter layer that accepts model-level config and internally provisions Parlant objects. Keep one canonical initialization path and remove stale kwargs.

findings.summary: Full `AgentToolsProtocol` is used where narrower tool contracts would reduce coupling.
evidence: `AgentToolsProtocol` composes six independent capabilities (messaging, participants, contacts, memory, schema export, dispatch) in `src/thenvoi/core/protocols.py`.
Adapters like Anthropic/LangGraph/Parlant type `tools` as full `AgentToolsProtocol` in `on_message`, forcing dependency on unrelated capabilities.
`PydanticAIAdapter` already demonstrates a narrower contract via `PydanticAIToolsProtocol` (messaging + dispatch), showing this decoupling is viable but inconsistently applied.
findings.suggestion: Define and adopt minimal per-adapter tool protocols (e.g., Messaging+Dispatch, Messaging+Schema, etc.) and update adapter signatures/tests/fakes to those slices. Keep `AgentToolsProtocol` only as a maximal implementation contract, not the default dependency type.

findings.summary: Codex test doubles violate the tool-schema protocol signature, weakening interface guarantees.
evidence: `ToolSchemaProviderProtocol.get_openai_tool_schemas` is declared as keyword-only `include_memory` in `src/thenvoi/core/protocols.py`.

`ToolSchemaFakeTools.get_openai_tool_schemas(self)` in `tests/adapters/test_codex_adapter.py` and `_ToolSchemaFakeTools.get_openai_tool_schemas(self)` in `tests/integrations/codex/test_adapter_e2e.py` omit that parameter.
`FakeAgentTools` in `src/thenvoi/testing/fake_tools.py` follows the full signature, so codex-specific fakes are inconsistent with the shared contract.
findings.suggestion: Align all test doubles to the protocol signature (`*, include_memory: bool = False`) and add a small protocol-conformance test for codex fakes so signature drift is caught before behavior tests run.

findings.summary: Adapter configuration abstractions are inconsistent, creating high-entropy constructor APIs.
evidence: `CodexAdapter` encapsulates options in `CodexAdapterConfig` (many fields but one bounded object) in `src/thenvoi/adapters/codex.py`.
Anthropic/ClaudeSDK/CrewAI/PydanticAI adapters expose many optional constructor args directly, mixing feature toggles, compatibility knobs, and provider settings in each class.
`CrewAIAdapter` still carries deprecated `system_prompt` compatibility behavior in constructor path, further widening and aging the surface.
findings.suggestion: Standardize on per-adapter typed config objects (Pydantic/dataclass) plus thin constructor overloads for backward compatibility; move deprecated aliases into explicit migration shims. This keeps adapter constructors stable and makes extension pressure explicit.

findings.summary: Bootstrap path couples env loading, credential lookup, and agent construction, making critical setup behavior hard to unit test deterministically.
evidence: `load_platform_urls()` conditionally calls `load_dotenv()` directly, tying behavior to process environment state.
`load_runtime_config()` always calls `resolve_agent_credentials(agent_key)` with no injected resolver.
`create_agent_from_runtime()` directly invokes `Agent.create(...)`, requiring heavy patching to test creation failure/success branches.
findings.suggestion: Introduce injectable collaborators (env loader, credential resolver, agent factory) via parameters with defaults; then add focused unit tests for each failure/success branch without monkeypatching global functions.

findings.summary: Session expiration logic depends on wall-clock reads in method bodies, which encourages brittle timing-dependent tests for TTL behavior.
evidence: `_evict_expired()` and `get_or_create()` both call `datetime.now(timezone.utc)` inline.
Eviction is opportunistic (runs on access methods), so tests must orchestrate call ordering and time progression to assert expiry.
No clock abstraction or overridable time source exists to make expiration scenarios deterministic.
findings.suggestion: Inject a clock callable (defaulting to UTC now) into `InMemorySessionStore` and centralize time acquisition through it; write deterministic tests by supplying a fake clock and explicit timestamps.

findings.summary: Handler API exposes only side effects and no outcome contract, increasing coupling and making tests assert implementation details.
evidence: `BaseHandler.handle(...)` returns `None`, so tests cannot assert structured routing/processing results.
The only observable output implied by the protocol is behavior through `AgentTools`, which usually requires mocks/spies and call-order assertions.
No standardized result/error object is defined to validate behavior independently of concrete tool invocation details.
findings.suggestion: Define a small handler result contract (e.g., handled/ignored/error metadata) and update implementations/tests to assert result semantics first, with tool-call assertions only where integration behavior is required.

findings.summary: Production-readiness claims are broader than the default quality gate coverage for integration contracts.
evidence: README.md labels many framework adapters as "Production ready".
pyproject.toml sets pytest `addopts = "-v -m 'not integration'"`, which excludes live integration checks by default.
tests/support/integration/config.py contains real API fixtures and policy enforcement intended for integration-mode runs.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a required CI contract matrix that runs integration suites (or targeted smoke contracts) per supported adapter extra, and align README production claims to that matrix (including explicit pass criteria and cadence).

findings.summary: Live fixture marker policy is robust but opt-in, leaving frequent test runs blind to integration contract regressions.
evidence: tests/support/integration/config.py enforces marker discipline in `pytest_runtest_setup` for live fixtures.
tests/support/integration/markers.py defines required marker decorators for env-gated integration tests.
pyproject.toml defaults to `not integration`, so these governance checks are bypassed unless explicitly enabled.
findings.suggestion: Add a mandatory lightweight integration contract stage (e.g., nightly/PR label-triggered) and fail builds when integration-marker policy violations exist, instead of relying on local opt-in execution.

findings.summary: Integration fixtures depend on external ordering/state, increasing flake risk for contract validation.
evidence: In `test_chat`, fixture behavior chooses the first peer from `list_agent_peers()` (`peers_response.data[0]`) when available.
In `test_peer_id`, fixture falls back to first peer if filtering cannot exclude owner.
README.md promotes broad framework usage, but this state-dependent selection weakens repeatable governance checks across environments.
findings.suggestion: Create deterministic fixture setup for integration tests (dedicated seeded peers/agents per run ID, explicit selection predicates, and fail-fast when preconditions are not met) to reduce environmental flakiness.

findings.summary: Docs-facing runtime contracts in heavily reused example modules are not paired with explicit contract tests in this governance slice.
evidence: examples/common/bootstrap.py defines shared bootstrap behavior for runtime credentials/endpoints.
examples/prompts/characters.py encodes strict behavioral rules (message limits, mission flow, tool usage constraints).
tests/support/events.py provides event factories that could support behavior-contract verification, but no explicit prompt/bootstrap contract assertions are present in this batch.
findings.suggestion: Add focused contract tests for example bootstrap and prompt invariants (credential resolution, endpoint precedence, required prompt sections/limits) and wire them into the default non-integration suite.

findings.summary: Codex adapter has become a multi-role orchestrator that mixes unrelated responsibilities and raises change risk.
evidence: One class handles RPC client bootstrapping, thread resume/start, turn loop, server request handling, local slash-commands, manual approval bookkeeping, model fallback, sandbox normalization, and event emission.

The adapter exposes many narrowly scoped private helpers that still depend on shared mutable adapter state (`_room_threads`, `_pending_approvals`, `_selected_model`, `_task_titles_by_id`), indicating high internal coupling instead of modular ownership.
Large behavior-specific test surface in `tests/adapters/test_codex_adapter.py` reflects this concentration by validating many orthogonal concerns through one entrypoint.
findings.suggestion: Split Codex behavior into composable collaborators (session lifecycle, command/approval controller, turn-event reducer, and policy/config normalizers) and keep `CodexAdapter` as orchestration glue with a narrow state model.

findings.summary: Framework adapters duplicate the same room bootstrap/system-update/history choreography with per-file drift.
evidence: Anthropic, PydanticAI, and CrewAI adapters each stage per-room history, inject participant/contact system updates, then run framework-specific execution loops with similar error-report pathways.
LangGraph adapter reimplements bootstrap-once logic (`_bootstrapped_rooms`) and system update injection independently, instead of using a shared adapter lifecycle component.
`AdapterLifecycleBase` centralizes only minimal conversion dispatch, leaving repeated runtime session policy spread across adapter files.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared room-session orchestration layer (bootstrap policy, update injection, error reporting contract) consumed by framework adapters, with framework-specific code limited to request/response translation.

findings.summary: Runtime execution context and platform runtime each aggregate too many lifecycle concerns, blurring ownership boundaries.
evidence: `ExecutionContext` handles queue processing, sync reconciliation with `/next`, retry accounting, stale-message recovery, participant cache, context hydration, and system-message injection in one class.
`PlatformRuntime` coordinates link startup/shutdown, metadata retrieval, contact strategy wiring, contact subscription state, broadcast queueing, and hub-room injection flow.
Contact handling integration requires cross-object callbacks (`_queue_broadcast`, `_inject_hub_event`, `_inject_hub_system_prompt`) that couple runtime orchestration to contact strategy internals.
findings.suggestion: Separate runtime into explicit components (message-sync engine, execution loop, participant/context cache, and contact orchestration facade) with clear handoff contracts to reduce cross-component callback coupling.

findings.summary: WebSocket channel APIs are mechanically repeated across streaming and link layers, increasing drift potential.
evidence: `WebSocketClient` repeats near-identical `join_*`/`leave_*` methods that differ mostly by topic string and handler map.
`ThenvoiLink` adds another repetitive layer for subscribe/unsubscribe and per-event wrapper methods (`_on_*`) that mostly translate payloads into event objects and enqueue them.
Duplicated choreography requires parallel edits whenever channel/event shapes change.
findings.suggestion: Move to a declarative channel registry (topic template + payload model + event constructor) and generate join/leave + dispatch wiring from one table shared by both layers.

findings.summary: Runner startup logic is duplicated across Docker/example entrypoints with inconsistent defaults and contracts.
evidence: All three runners perform similar config-path/env resolution, mount validation, prompt composition, adapter creation, and lifecycle startup with hand-maintained variations.
Behavior diverges across files (mount requirements, role/workspace resolution style, inclusion of repo-init context bundle), despite serving the same startup shape.
This creates a maintenance pattern where feature additions/fixes must be manually replicated across entrypoints.
findings.suggestion: Define a single declarative runner spec and shared builder in `runner_core` that each entrypoint configures via small profile objects rather than duplicating startup flow.

findings.summary: Gateway history participant reconstruction relies on a sender-type convention that drifts from other runtime usage.
evidence: `GatewayHistoryConverter` tracks participants only when `sender_type == "agent"` (lowercase), while other runtime paths compare against `"Agent"` (capitalized) for agent-origin filtering.
No gateway-focused integration assertion in `tests/integration/test_history_converters.py` covers this sender-type normalization boundary, so convention drift can persist silently.
This is a coherence issue at the contract boundary: converter behavior depends on implicit casing assumptions not normalized in one place.
findings.suggestion: Normalize sender type through a shared canonical helper (case-insensitive enum-style normalization) and add converter integration tests that cover mixed-case sender types from platform history.
